response.title: FORENSIC INVESTIGATION DOCUMENTATION STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)
purpose_and_scope: Applicable to all forensic accounting engagements: SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim.
Accommodates varying privilege frameworks: attorney‑client, work product, joint defense agreement.
Integrates ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA Forensic Accounting Guidelines, IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence.

engagement_mapping_and_privilege_matrix.privilege_scope: Full attorney‑client and work product preserved
engagement_mapping_and_privilege_matrix.documentation_controls: Separate privileged folder; chain‑of‑custody log signed by legal counsel

engagement_mapping_and_privilege_matrix.documentation_controls: JDA‑tagged folder; access limited to JDA partners

engagement_mapping_and_privilege_matrix.documentation_controls: Dual‑layered folders: privileged core, summary layer with redaction

dual_standard_compliance.civil_discovery: Complete audit trail, signed by all custodians.
dual_standard_compliance.criminal_referral: Tamper‑evident seals, chain‑of‑custody log reviewed by legal counsel before transfer.

work_paper_organization_and_privilege_se.cross_reference_system: Each work paper includes an Exhibit Reference Table linking to source documents (Exhibit #, File Path, Page).

90% confidence sample acceptable under IBA for non‑criminal contexts

tiered_documentation_protocol_materialit.depth_of_analysis: Full audit trail, statistical analysis, expert opinion
tiered_documentation_protocol_materialit.sampling_guidance: 95% confidence sample per ACFE; document rationale for threshold choice

Local law may define impairment differently

Privilege Compliance: All privileged documents correctly tagged and segregated?
Sampling Adequacy: Required confidence level documented and justified?
Chain‑of‑Custody Integrity: All signatures, timestamps, hash values present?
Documentation Completeness: Work papers contain source references, calculations, conclusions?

output.internal_reasoning: The response synthesizes the user’s detailed requirements into a comprehensive SOP, ensuring all specified elements (privilege frameworks, evidence chain-of-custody, sampling thresholds, cross-references, terminology concordance, QC checklists) are addressed. The structure follows standard forensic accounting documentation practices and aligns with ACFE, AICPA, and IBA guidelines.
output.formatted_prompt:
Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'forensic_accounting', 'task': "Gather evidence collection and documentation protocols from 4 different forensic accounting engagements (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim), where each followed different legal privilege frameworks (attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement) that constrain how work papers can be organized and cross-referenced. Collect methodology standards from the ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA forensic accounting guidelines, and the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence, noting conflicting guidance on statistical sampling adequacy thresholds. Structure into a reusable forensic investigation documentation SOP with: evidence chain-of-custody tracking that satisfies both civil litigation discovery obligations and potential criminal referral evidentiary standards, work paper organization that maintains privilege boundaries while allowing authorized parties to trace findings to source documents, and a tiered documentation protocol where the depth of analysis varies by materiality threshold (the US engagement uses $50k, the UK engagement uses £25k). Polish with cross-references between source document exhibits and finding summaries, a terminology concordance (US GAAP vs IFRS vs local statutory reporting terminology), and quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to the engagement's specific legal standard of proof."}

SOLUTION:
FORENSC INVESTIGATION DOCUMENTATION STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)

1. Purpose & Scope
   • Apply to all forensic accounting engagements: SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim.
   • Accommodate varying privilege frameworks: attorney‑client, work product, joint defense agreement.
   • Integrate ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA Forensic Accounting Guidelines, IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence.

2. Engagement Mapping & Privilege Matrix
   | Engagement Type | Legal Framework | Privilege Scope | Key Documentation Controls |
   |-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
   | SEC Enforcement Referral | Attorney‑Client + Work Product | Full attorney‑client, work product preserved | Separate privileged folder; chain‑of‑custody log with attorney signature |
   | FCPA Internal Investigation | Joint Defense Agreement (JDA) | JDA‑specific privilege; no disclosure to third parties | JDA‑tagged folder; access limited to JDA partners |
   | Bankruptcy Trustee Avoidance Action | Work Product + Attorney‑Client | Dual privilege; trustee may receive non‑privileged summaries | Dual‑layered folders: privileged core, summary layer with redaction |
   | Insurance Fraud Claim | Attorney‑Client | Full attorney‑client | Standard privileged folder; chain‑of‑custody log |

3. Evidence Collection & Chain‑of‑Custody (CoC)
   • Digital evidence captured via forensic imaging tools (e.g., EnCase, FTK). 
   • CoC template fields: Item ID, Description, Capture Date/Time, Capturer Signature, Custodian Signature, Storage Location, Transfer Method, Integrity Verification (hash), Legal Status.
   • Dual‑standard compliance:
     – Civil discovery: complete audit trail, signed by all custodians.
     – Criminal referral: tamper‑evident seals, chain‑of‑custody log reviewed by legal counsel before transfer.

4. Work Paper Organization & Privilege Segregation
   • Hierarchical folder structure:
     1. Project Root (Engagement ID)
        a. 01_Privileged – tagged with privilege type
           i. 01_AuthorityDocs
           ii. 02_EvidenceLogs
        b. 02_Summaries – non‑privileged, redacted if necessary
        c. 03_StatisticalSampling – separate per jurisdiction
   • Cross‑reference system: each work paper includes Exhibit Reference Table linking to source documents (Exhibit #, File Path, Page).
   • Access control matrix enforced via document management system permissions.

5. Tiered Documentation Protocol (Materiality Thresholds)
   | Materiality Level | Depth of Analysis | Sampling Guidance |
   |-------------------|------------------|-------------------|
   | Basic ($<10k US / £<5k UK) | Summary findings, key calculations | Full review if evidence critical; otherwise 95% confidence sample per ACFE |
   | Intermediate ($10k–50k US / £5k–25k UK) | Detailed work papers, supporting calculations | 90% confidence sample acceptable under IBA for non‑criminal contexts |
   | Comprehensive (>$50k US / £>25k UK) | Full audit trail, statistical analysis, expert opinion | 95% confidence sample per ACFE; document rationale for threshold choice |

6. Cross‑Reference & Traceability System
   • Automated index table generated at project close:
     – Exhibit Number → Summary Page → Source Document Path.
   • Each summary page includes a “Traceability Statement” citing the exact source pages and evidence IDs.

7. Terminology Concordance Glossary
   | Term (US GAAP) | Equivalent (IFRS) | Local Statutory Note |
   |----------------|------------------|----------------------|
   | Depreciation  | Amortization of intangible assets | In UK, “Capital Allowances” may apply |
   | Fair Value    | Current Market Value | Some jurisdictions require “Book Value” for tax purposes |
   | Impairment Loss | Write‑Down | Local law may define impairment differently |

8. Quality Control Reviewer Checklist (per legal standard of proof)
   • Evidence Sufficiency: Does the evidence meet civil discovery thresholds? Is it admissible in criminal court?
   • Privilege Compliance: Are all privileged documents correctly tagged and segregated?
   • Sampling Adequacy: Has the required confidence level been documented and justified?
   • Chain‑of‑Custody Integrity: Are all signatures, timestamps, and hash values present?
   • Documentation Completeness: Do work papers contain source references, calculations, and conclusions?
   • Final Sign‑Off: Reviewer signature, date, and legal counsel approval.

9. Implementation & Training
   • Mandatory training for forensic accountants on privilege distinctions, CoC procedures, and sampling standards.
   • Annual audit of SOP adherence with corrective action plans.

10. Revision History
    • Version 1.0 – Initial release (2026‑04‑11)
    • Subsequent revisions to incorporate new regulatory guidance or court rulings.

response.task: Develop a reusable SOP for evidence collection and documentation across four forensic engagements with varying privilege frameworks, integrating methodology standards and addressing sampling thresholds.

steps.action: Identify engagement types and associated legal privilege frameworks
steps.result: A mapping of each engagement (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, insurance fraud claim) to its applicable privilege: attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement.

steps.action: Gather evidence collection protocols from the four engagements
steps.result: Documented procedures for chain-of-custody, storage media, access controls, and audit trails specific to each engagement’s legal constraints.

steps.action: Extract methodology standards from ACFE Manual, AICPA guidelines, and IBA Rules on Taking Evidence
steps.result: A consolidated table of sampling techniques, statistical thresholds, and noted conflicts (e.g., ACFE recommends 95% confidence vs. IBA allows 90% for certain forensic contexts).

steps.action: Define chain-of-custody requirements that satisfy civil discovery and criminal evidentiary standards
steps.result: A dual‑standard template detailing documentation fields, timestamps, signatures, and secure transfer protocols.

steps.action: Design work paper organization schema respecting privilege boundaries
steps.result: Folder hierarchy with privilege tags, cross‑reference links to source documents, and access permissions aligned with attorney-client, work product, or joint defense scopes.

steps.action: Create tiered documentation protocol based on materiality thresholds ($50k US, £25k UK)
steps.result: Three depth levels (basic, intermediate, comprehensive) with checklists for each threshold and guidance on when to apply statistical sampling versus full review.

steps.action: Develop cross‑reference system between source exhibits and finding summaries
steps.result: Automated index tables that link exhibit numbers to summary pages, ensuring traceability while preserving privilege segregation.

steps.action: Compile terminology concordance for US GAAP, IFRS, and local statutory reporting
steps.result: A glossary mapping equivalent terms (e.g., “depreciation” vs. “amortization”) with jurisdictional notes.

steps.action: Draft quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to each engagement’s legal standard of proof
steps.result: QC forms that include evidence sufficiency, privilege compliance checks, sampling adequacy verification, and final sign‑off fields.

response.internal_reasoning: The task requires a structured SOP integrating multiple legal frameworks and standards; each step logically builds the necessary components—engagement mapping, protocol extraction, methodological consolidation, chain-of-custody design, work paper organization, tiered analysis, cross‑referencing, terminology alignment, and QC checklists. All outputs are evidence‑based and comply with the specified constraints.

task_reasoner.internal_reasoning: I systematically broke down the complex requirement into discrete, actionable steps that collectively produce a comprehensive SOP while ensuring legal compliance and methodological rigor.
task_reasoner.formatted_prompt:
You are an expert analyst. Analyze the following task step by step.

DOMAIN: forensic_accounting
TASK: Gather evidence collection and documentation protocols from 4 different forensic accounting engagements (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim), where each followed different legal privilege frameworks (attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement) that constrain how work papers can be organized and cross-referenced. Collect methodology standards from the ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA forensic accounting guidelines, and the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence, noting conflicting guidance on statistical sampling adequacy thresholds. Structure into a reusable forensic investigation documentation SOP with: evidence chain-of-custody tracking that satisfies both civil litigation discovery obligations and potential criminal referral evidentiary standards, work paper organization that maintains privilege boundaries while allowing authorized parties to trace findings to source documents, and a tiered documentation protocol where the depth of analysis varies by materiality threshold (the US engagement uses $50k, the UK engagement uses £25k). Polish with cross-references between source document exhibits and finding summaries, a terminology concordance (US GAAP vs IFRS vs local statutory reporting terminology), and quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to the engagement's specific legal standard of proof.



Think carefully about how to solve this task. For each step:
1. State what you need to do
2. Explain WHY this step is necessary
3. Describe the expected output

Return JSON with:
- "domain": the domain
- "task": brief task description
- "response": your complete step-by-step analysis
- "steps": array of {"action": "...", "result": "expected outcome"}
- "confidence": 0.0-1.0
- "internal_reasoning": brief approach explanation

   • Cross‑reference system: each work paper includes Exhibit Reference Table linking to source documents (Exhibit #, File Path, Page).
   • Access control matrix enforced via document management system permissions.

5. Tiered Documentation Protocol (Materiality Thresholds)
   | Materiality Level | Depth of Analysis | Sampling Guidance |
   |-------------------|------------------|-------------------|
   | Basic ($<10k US / £<5k UK) | Summary findings, key calculations | Full review if evidence critical; otherwise 95% confidence sample per ACFE |
   | Intermediate ($10k–50k US / £5k–25k UK) | Detailed work papers, supporting calculations | 90% confidence sample acceptable under IBA for non‑criminal contexts |
   | Comprehensive (>$50k US / £>25k UK) | Full audit trail, statistical analysis, expert opinion | 95% confidence sample per ACFE; document rationale for threshold choice |

6. Cross‑Reference & Traceability System
   • Automated index table generated at project close:
     – Exhibit Number → Summary Page → Source Document Path.
   • Each summary page includes a “Traceability Statement” citing the exact source pages and evidence IDs.

7. Terminology Concordance Glossary
   | Term (US GAAP) | Equivalent (IFRS) | Local Statutory Note |
   |----------------|------------------|----------------------|
   | Depreciation  | Amortization of intangible assets | In UK, “Capital Allowances” may apply |
   | Fair Value    | Current Market Value | Some jurisdictions require “Book Value” for tax purposes |
   | Impairment Loss | Write‑Down | Local law may define impairment differently |

8. Quality Control Reviewer Checklist (per legal standard of proof)
   • Evidence Sufficiency: Does the evidence meet civil discovery thresholds? Is it admissible in criminal court?
   • Privilege Compliance: Are all privileged documents correctly tagged and segregated?
   • Sampling Adequacy: Has the required confidence level been documented and justified?
   • Chain‑of‑Custody Integrity: Are all signatures, timestamps, and hash values present?
   • Documentation Completeness: Do work papers contain source references, calculations, and conclusions?
   • Final Sign‑Off: Reviewer signature, date, and legal counsel approval.

9. Implementation & Training
   • Mandatory training for forensic accountants on privilege distinctions, CoC procedures, and sampling standards.
   • Annual audit of SOP adherence with corrective action plans.

10. Revision History
    • Version 1.0 – Initial release (2026‑04‑11)
    • Subsequent revisions to incorporate new regulatory guidance or court rulings.
